chunks.text: Travel & Expense Policy — Overview Document: TEP-001 Version: 3.2 Effective Date: January 1, 2025 Owner: Finance Operations Applies To: All Northwind Logistics employees, contractors, and authorized agents

sections.section_heading: Purpose

chunks.text: This policy establishes the rules and procedures governing business travel and the reimbursement of business expenses incurred by employees of Northwind Logistics ("the Company"). All employees are responsible for understanding and complying with this policy.

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy applies to all expenses incurred in the course of business travel, client entertainment, conference attendance, and ordinary business operations where Company funds are committed or reimbursed.

sections.section_heading: Business purpose

chunks.text: Business purpose.
Every reimbursable expense must have a clear and documented business purpose. The Company does not reimburse personal expenses under any circumstances.

sections.section_heading: Reasonableness

chunks.text: Reasonableness. Expenses must be reasonable, necessary, and incurred at the lowest practical cost consistent with effective business performance.

sections.section_heading: Documentation

chunks.text: Documentation. All expenses must be supported by original itemized receipts as detailed in the Receipt Requirements Policy (TEP-007).

sections.section_heading: Timeliness

chunks.text: Timeliness.
Expense reports must be submitted within 30 days of the end of the trip. Expenses submitted more than 60 days after they were incurred will not be reimbursed except by VP-level exception.

sections.section_heading: Expenses up to $1,000 per submission: direct manager approva

chunks.text: Expenses up to $1,000 per submission: direct manager approval.

sections.section_heading: Approval thresholds are cumulative per submission, not per l

chunks.text: Approval thresholds are cumulative per submission, not per line item.

sections.section_heading: Related policies

chunks.text: This policy is supplemented by the following, each of which controls in the area of its subject matter: • TEP-002 Meals and Entertainment Policy • TEP-003 Alcohol Policy • TEP-004 Lodging Policy • TEP-005 Air Travel Policy • TEP-006 Ground Transportation Policy • TEP-007 Receipt Requirements Policy • TEP-008 Per-Diem Rates • TEP-009 Employee Grades Reference • TEP-010 Corporate Card Policy • TEP-013 International Travel Policy • TEP-014 Conference Attendance Policy

sections.section_heading: Finance Operations conducts random and risk-based audits of

chunks.text: Finance Operations conducts random and risk-based audits of submitted expense reports. Selected submissions will require additional documentation.

sections.section_heading: Violations of this policy may result in non-reimbursement, r

chunks.text: Violations of this policy may result in non-reimbursement, requirement to repay, or disciplinary action up to and including termination, depending on severity and intent.

sections.section_heading: Repeat or willful violations are escalated to HR and the rel

chunks.text: Repeat or willful violations are escalated to HR and the relevant business unit head.

sections.section_heading: Exceptions

chunks.text: Exceptions to this policy require written approval from the CFO or designated delegate prior to incurring the expense. Retroactive exception requests are strongly discouraged and granted only in genuine emergencies.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: For purposes of this policy and the broader Travel & Expense framework: • Reimbursable — Eligible for repayment by the Company subject to compliance with applicable policy and supporting documentation. • VP approval — Written approval from a Grade 9 employee or above per TEP-009 §3.2. Email is sufficient unless otherwise specified. • Approved booking tool — Concur Travel, the Company's contracted travel booking platform. • Corporate Card — A payment card issued in the employee's name under the Corporate Card Policy (TEP-010). • Submission — An expense report submitted via Concur or successor system, comprising one or more line items each supported by appropriate documentation. • Audit — Random or risk-based review of submitted expense reports by Finance Operations, supplemented by external audit as required.

sections.section_heading: Related documents

chunks.text: This policy anchors the Northwind Logistics Travel & Expense framework. Related documents: • TEP-002 Meals and Entertainment Policy • TEP-003 Alcohol Policy • TEP-004 Lodging Policy • TEP-005 Air Travel Policy • TEP-006 Ground Transportation Policy • TEP-007 Receipt Requirements Policy • TEP-008 Per-Diem Rates • TEP-009 Employee Grades Reference • TEP-010 Corporate Card Policy • TEP-012 Gifts and Entertainment Policy • TEP-013 International Travel Policy • TEP-014 Conference Attendance Policy • COC-001 Code of Conduct • REC-001 Records Retention Schedule (for retention of expense documentation) • SEC-301 Travel Risk and Security Protocol (for international travel)

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy governs the reimbursement of meal expenses incurred during business travel, in-office working meals, and client entertainment. Alcohol-related rules are addressed separately in TEP-003 (Alcohol Policy).

sections.section_heading: Per-meal caps for individual business travel

chunks.text: Meal expenses incurred by an employee traveling on business, when dining alone or with other Northwind employees, are subject to the following per-person caps (inclusive of tax and tip): Meal Cap (USD) Breakfast $25 Lunch $35 Dinner $75

sections.section_heading: The caps in Section 2 apply per person per meal

chunks.text: The caps in Section 2 apply per person per meal. Multiple meals stacked under one receipt (e.g., a brunch combining breakfast and lunch) are subject to the higher applicable cap.

sections.section_heading: Expenses above these caps are not reimbursable except under

chunks.text: Expenses above these caps are not reimbursable except under Section 4 (Client Entertainment) or Section 5 (Exceptions).

sections.section_heading: High-cost cities

chunks.text: High-cost cities. Caps are increased by 25% for travel in Tier 1 cities (see Lodging Policy TEP-004 §3 for the current Tier 1 list).

sections.section_heading: Tips

chunks.text: Tips above 20% of the pre-tax meal total are considered personal and are not reimbursable.

sections.section_heading: Meal expenses incurred while hosting clients, prospects, or

chunks.text: Meal expenses incurred while hosting clients, prospects, or external partners are governed by separate caps: • Lunch: up to $80 per person • Dinner: up to $150 per person

sections.section_heading: Client entertainment expenses above $100 per person require

chunks.text: Client entertainment expenses above $100 per person require prior written approval from a Vice President or higher.

sections.section_heading: The employee must record on the receipt or expense report:

chunks.text: • Names of all attendees • The business purpose of the meeting • The name of the company or organization represented by each external attendee

sections.section_heading: Entertainment of family members, friends, or non-business co

chunks.text: Entertainment of family members, friends, or non-business contacts is never reimbursable, regardless of who else is present.

sections.section_heading: Where a meal is part of a conference, training, or off-site

chunks.text: Where a meal is part of a conference, training, or off-site whose registration fee includes meals, the registration fee covers the meal and no separate meal reimbursement is permitted.

sections.section_heading: Where a meal is unavailable at or below the cap due to extra

chunks.text: Where a meal is unavailable at or below the cap due to extraordinary circumstances (e.g., the only open restaurant in a small town has a $90 dinner menu), the employee may submit the actual cost with written explanation; reimbursement requires manager approval.

sections.section_heading: Standing exceptions are not granted

chunks.text: Standing exceptions are not granted. Each exception is reviewed individually.

sections.section_heading: Non-reimbursable categories

chunks.text: The following are never reimbursable as meal expenses: • Alcoholic beverages on solo travel (see TEP-003) • Mini-bar items from hotel rooms • Meals for accompanying spouses, family members, or guests not on company business • "Recovery" or "team morale" meals not pre-approved by manager

sections.section_heading: "My breakfast at the hotel was charged as $32 because of a '

chunks.text: "My breakfast at the hotel was charged as $32 because of a 'destination resort breakfast' fee. Is this reimbursable?" The mandatory portion bundled into a daily resort fee is reimbursable as part of the room rate (see TEP-004 §4.2).
The breakfast itself counts against the breakfast cap; reasonable overages within the resort context may be approved by your manager.

sections.section_heading: "I attended a team off-site where one Northwind colleague is

chunks.text: "I attended a team off-site where one Northwind colleague is technically a contractor. Does that count as client entertainment?" No. Contractors paid by the Company are not external clients for purposes of this policy. Apply standard per-meal caps.

sections.section_heading: "The check arrived late and I tipped the server in cash beyo

chunks.text: "The check arrived late and I tipped the server in cash beyond what's on the receipt. Is the cash tip reimbursable?" The tip must be reflected on the receipt or submitted with a Missing Receipt Affidavit (TEP-007 §4.2) describing the cash portion. Reimbursement remains capped at 20% per Section 3.

sections.section_heading: "My client picked up the check

chunks.text: "My client picked up the check.
Can I expense my entertainment of them at our next meeting instead?" Yes, subject to the standard caps and approval requirements. Each entertainment event is evaluated independently.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Meal — Breakfast, lunch, or dinner consumed in the course of business travel or business activity. Snacks, coffees, and small purchases under $10 are not "meals" for cap purposes and are tracked as incidentals. • Client entertainment — A meal or event hosted by a Company employee for one or more external clients, prospects, or business partners, with the primary purpose of relationship development. • Itemized receipt — A receipt showing each individual item or charge, as defined in TEP-007 §3. • High-cost city — A city in Lodging Tier 1 per TEP-004 §3. The current Tier 1 list is the controlling list for meal-cap purposes.

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy governs the reimbursement of alcoholic beverages charged to the Company, whether on a Corporate Card, personal card with reimbursement request, or in any other form.

sections.section_heading: General rule

chunks.text: Alcoholic beverages are reimbursable only when incurred during sanctioned client entertainment.

sections.section_heading: "Sanctioned client entertainment" means a meal or event host

chunks.text: "Sanctioned client entertainment" means a meal or event hosted by a Northwind employee for one or more external clients, prospects, or business partners, where the primary purpose is the development or maintenance of a business relationship.

sections.section_heading: Sanctioned client entertainment involving alcohol requires:

chunks.text: • At least one external attendee to be physically present • Prior approval from a Vice President or higher, documented in writing (email is sufficient) • A per-person alcohol spend that does not exceed $50

sections.section_heading: Prohibited charges

chunks.text: The following alcohol-related charges are never reimbursable, even if charged on the Corporate Card:

sections.section_heading: Solo travel

chunks.text: Solo travel.
Any alcoholic beverage purchased while traveling on business without external clients present. This includes drinks at hotel bars, with meals taken alone, or at airport lounges, regardless of amount.

sections.section_heading: Team-only meals

chunks.text: Team-only meals. Alcohol consumed at meals attended only by Northwind employees, including offsite dinners, holiday gatherings (unless explicitly designated as a Company event with HR approval), and team morale events.

sections.section_heading: In-room consumption

chunks.text: In-room consumption. Alcohol purchased through hotel room service or mini-bars under any circumstances.

sections.section_heading: Excess of cap

chunks.text: Excess of cap. The portion of any alcohol charge exceeding the $50 per-person cap defined in Section 2.2.

sections.section_heading: Personal consumption disguised as a meal

chunks.text: Personal consumption disguised as a meal. Alcohol charges where the food portion of the receipt is minimal (less than 50% of the receipt total) will be reviewed for compliance and may be denied.

sections.section_heading: When alcohol is included on a reimbursable receipt, the alco

chunks.text: When alcohol is included on a reimbursable receipt, the alcohol portion must be itemized separately on the expense report, even when bundled with food on the same receipt.

sections.section_heading: The expense report must identify:

chunks.text: • All attendees (employees and externals) • The VP-level approver for the entertainment • The business purpose

sections.section_heading: If a Corporate Card charge for alcohol is found to violate t

chunks.text: If a Corporate Card charge for alcohol is found to violate this policy in audit, the employee is responsible for repaying the Company within 30 days of notification.

sections.section_heading: Repeated violations are referred to HR for disciplinary revi

chunks.text: Repeated violations are referred to HR for disciplinary review.

sections.section_heading: Local law

chunks.text: In jurisdictions where alcohol consumption or purchase is restricted by law (e.g., dry counties, certain international destinations), this policy yields to local law and no alcohol expenses will be reimbursed regardless of business context.

sections.section_heading: Solo dinner with a beer

chunks.text: Solo dinner with a beer. Employee A on a solo trip to Denver orders a $58 dinner including one $9 beer.
The $9 alcohol charge is not reimbursable per §3.1. The $49 food portion remains reimbursable subject to TEP-002 §2 caps. The line item should be flagged and only the food portion reimbursed.

sections.section_heading: Working dinner with two Northwind colleagues

chunks.text: Working dinner with two Northwind colleagues. Three Northwind employees dine together while traveling for the same project.
Alcohol is not reimbursable per §3.2 (team-only meal). The food portion is reimbursable subject to per-person caps in TEP-002 §2.

sections.section_heading: Pre-arranged client entertainment

chunks.text: Pre-arranged client entertainment. A Director and a Senior Manager host two external customers for dinner with prior VP approval documented by email.
Alcohol per person is reimbursable up to $50, food up to the entertainment cap in TEP-002 §4. Receipt must itemize alcohol separately per §4.1.

sections.section_heading: Conference attendee reception

chunks.text: Conference attendee reception. A conference reception included drinks as part of the registration.
No incremental alcohol charge to the Company occurs; the registration fee is reimbursable under TEP-014.

sections.section_heading: Hotel mini-bar wine

chunks.text: Hotel mini-bar wine. Never reimbursable, regardless of business context (§3.3).

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Alcohol — Any beverage containing ethyl alcohol at a concentration greater than 0.5% by volume.

Includes beer, wine, spirits, hard seltzer, and prepared cocktails. • Sanctioned client entertainment — As defined in §2.1. • External attendee — A person who is not a current employee of Northwind Logistics or its affiliates. Contractors and consultants paid by the Company are not external attendees. • Itemization — A receipt showing alcohol charges separately from food charges, sufficient to identify the alcohol portion as a discrete amount.

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy governs the reimbursement of lodging expenses (hotels, short-term rentals, and equivalent) incurred during business travel.

sections.section_heading: Lodging should be booked through the Company's approved book

chunks.text: Lodging should be booked through the Company's approved booking tool (Concur Travel) whenever practical. Bookings outside the tool require manager approval and a brief justification.

sections.section_heading: Employees should book lodging at least 14 days in advance wh

chunks.text: Employees should book lodging at least 14 days in advance whenever the trip is planned that far ahead. Last-minute bookings (under 7 days) require manager approval if the rate exceeds the applicable cap.

sections.section_heading: City tier rate caps

chunks.text: Maximum reimbursable nightly rate (room rate plus mandatory taxes and resort fees; excludes incidentals): Tier 1 — $350 per night New York, NY; San Francisco, CA; Boston, MA; Washington, DC; Los Angeles, CA; Seattle, WA; London, UK; Zurich, CH; Tokyo, JP; Singapore, SG. Tier 2 — $250 per night Chicago, IL; Denver, CO; Atlanta, GA; Austin, TX; Dallas, TX; Houston, TX; Miami, FL; Portland, OR; San Diego, CA; Toronto, ON; Amsterdam, NL; Berlin, DE; Sydney, AU. Tier 3 — $175 per night All other locations within the United States and Canada. Tier 4 — Submit for approval International destinations not listed above.
Rate cap is set at the time of booking based on prevailing local rates and approved by Finance Operations.

sections.section_heading: Standard room or equivalent is the default category

chunks.text: Standard room or equivalent is the default category. Suites, club-level rooms, and view upgrades are not reimbursable unless they are the lowest available rate in the property or the upgrade is offered at no additional charge.

sections.section_heading: Short-term rentals (Airbnb, VRBO) are permitted at the appli

chunks.text: Short-term rentals (Airbnb, VRBO) are permitted at the applicable tier cap. Cleaning fees and service fees count toward the cap.

sections.section_heading: Incidentals not reimbursable

chunks.text: The following are never reimbursable as lodging: • Mini-bar consumption (food or alcohol) • In-room movies, video games, or other entertainment • Spa, gym, or recreation surcharges (gym is reimbursable only if the hotel has a mandatory daily resort/gym fee, in which case it counts toward the room cap) • Laundry, except for trips exceeding 5 consecutive nights • Personal phone calls

sections.section_heading: Where the city tier cap cannot be met at any available prope

chunks.text: Where the city tier cap cannot be met at any available property (e.g., a conference has driven up rates citywide), the employee may submit the actual cost with manager approval and a screenshot or written explanation showing comparable rates.

sections.section_heading: Cancellation charges for legitimate business reasons (trip c

chunks.text: Cancellation charges for legitimate business reasons (trip cancelled by manager, illness, family emergency) are reimbursable.

sections.section_heading: No-show or late-cancellation charges due to employee oversig

chunks.text: No-show or late-cancellation charges due to employee oversight are not reimbursable.

sections.section_heading: Tier 1 cap exceeded due to citywide event

chunks.text: Tier 1 cap exceeded due to citywide event.

A conference in San Francisco has driven hotel rates citywide to $480/nt. The Tier 1 cap is $350/nt. The employee may book the lowest available rate and submit screenshots of comparable rates with manager approval, per §6.1. Without that supporting documentation, the excess over $350/nt is not reimbursable.

sections.section_heading: Tier 2 city, premium hotel selected for convenience

chunks.text: Tier 2 city, premium hotel selected for convenience.

Employee books the Hyatt Regency Chicago at $315/nt; cap is $250. Excess is not reimbursable unless a lower-cost alternative was demonstrably unavailable. Manager should encourage selection within cap absent an unusual circumstance.

sections.section_heading: Tier 1 city, room with view upgrade auto-applied

chunks.text: Tier 1 city, room with view upgrade auto-applied. Employee did not request an upgrade but received one at check-in (no additional charge). Reimbursable as the lowest available rate.

sections.section_heading: Short-term rental

chunks.text: Short-term rental.

Employee selects an Airbnb at $190/nt + $75 cleaning fee + $20 service fee = $285 for one night in Atlanta (Tier 2, cap $250). The cleaning and service fees count toward the cap per §4.2. The submission exceeds the cap by $35 and is flagged.

sections.section_heading: Mandatory resort fee

chunks.text: Mandatory resort fee. Hotel bills $20/nt mandatory resort fee that includes Wi-Fi and gym.
This counts toward the cap per §3 (rate caps include mandatory taxes and resort fees).

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Nightly rate — The base room rate plus all mandatory taxes, occupancy fees, tourism assessments, and resort/destination fees, divided by nights stayed. Optional incidentals (parking, laundry beyond §5 exceptions, mini-bar) are not part of the nightly rate. • Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3 / Tier 4 — City classifications per §3, used for lodging rate caps and per-diem rates. • Concur Travel — The Company's approved booking tool.
References to "approved booking tool" mean Concur Travel. • Short-term rental — Lodging provided by Airbnb, VRBO, or equivalent platform.

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy governs the reimbursement of air travel expenses (commercial passenger flights) for business purposes.

sections.section_heading: Economy class is the default for all domestic and short-haul

chunks.text: Economy class is the default for all domestic and short-haul international flights. Domestic flights are flights wholly within the contiguous United States, Alaska, or Hawaii.

sections.section_heading: Premium economy (or equivalent) is permitted on any single f

chunks.text: Premium economy (or equivalent) is permitted on any single flight segment with a scheduled duration of 6 hours or more. The premium economy upgrade does not require additional approval if the flight meets the duration threshold.

sections.section_heading: Business class is permitted only on international flight seg

chunks.text: Business class is permitted only on international flight segments with a scheduled duration of 10 hours or more, and requires prior written approval from a Vice President or higher.

sections.section_heading: Flights should be booked through Concur Travel whenever prac

chunks.text: Flights should be booked through Concur Travel whenever practical.

sections.section_heading: Flights should be booked at least 14 days in advance wheneve

chunks.text: Flights should be booked at least 14 days in advance whenever the trip is planned that far ahead. Last-minute bookings (under 7 days) require manager approval.

sections.section_heading: Employees should book the lowest reasonable fare consistent

chunks.text: Employees should book the lowest reasonable fare consistent with the business need, considering: • Direct flights are preferred when the price difference is less than $200 vs a one-stop itinerary • Schedule constraints (cannot arrive after 11pm the night before a morning meeting) • Departure flexibility (avoid red-eyes unless required by the business)

sections.section_heading: Checked baggage

chunks.text: Checked baggage. First checked bag is reimbursable. Second checked bag requires manager approval and a business justification.

sections.section_heading: Seat selection

chunks.text: Seat selection. Standard seat selection (window/aisle/middle) is reimbursable.
Extra-legroom seats are reimbursable for flights of 4 hours or more, or for employees with a documented accommodation need.

sections.section_heading: Change and cancellation fees are reimbursable when the chang

chunks.text: Change and cancellation fees are reimbursable when the change is for a documented business reason. Changes for personal preference are not reimbursable.

sections.section_heading: In-flight Wi-Fi is reimbursable if used for business purpose

chunks.text: In-flight Wi-Fi is reimbursable if used for business purposes.

sections.section_heading: Employees may retain frequent-flyer miles and points earned

chunks.text: Employees may retain frequent-flyer miles and points earned on business travel.

sections.section_heading: Employees may not select higher-cost flights solely to accum

chunks.text: Employees may not select higher-cost flights solely to accumulate miles. The lowest reasonable fare governs.

sections.section_heading: Travel costs for spouses, family members, or other companion

chunks.text: Travel costs for spouses, family members, or other companions are never reimbursable, even when they accompany the employee on a business trip.

sections.section_heading: If a business itinerary is extended for personal reasons (e.

chunks.text: If a business itinerary is extended for personal reasons (e.g., a weekend before or after meetings), only the airfare cost that would have been incurred for the business portion is reimbursable. Employees should obtain a comparison quote at booking time.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Domestic flight — A flight wholly within the contiguous United States, Alaska, or Hawaii. • Short-haul international — An international flight segment with scheduled duration less than 6 hours. • Long-haul international — An international flight segment with scheduled duration 6 hours or more. • Class of service — The fare class booked: Economy (Main Cabin / Coach), Premium Economy (Comfort+, Economy Plus, Premium Select, etc.), Business, or First. Names vary by carrier; the class category controls. • Comparison quote — A screenshot or saved itinerary showing the alternative fare that would have been incurred without the personal extension or upgrade.

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy governs the reimbursement of ground transportation expenses incurred during business travel, including rideshare, taxis, rental cars, public transit, personal vehicle mileage, and parking.

sections.section_heading: Rideshare services (Uber, Lyft, and regional equivalents) ar

chunks.text: Rideshare services (Uber, Lyft, and regional equivalents) are reimbursable for business-related transportation, including airport transfers, transit between meetings, and end-of-day return to lodging.

sections.section_heading: Standard service categories (UberX, Lyft Standard, regional

chunks.text: Standard service categories (UberX, Lyft Standard, regional equivalents) should be selected. Premium categories (Uber Black, Lyft Lux, etc.) are not reimbursable unless they are the only available option, in which case a written explanation is required.

sections.section_heading: Taxis are reimbursable at the metered fare plus reasonable t

chunks.text: Taxis are reimbursable at the metered fare plus reasonable tip (up to 20% of the fare).

sections.section_heading: Rental cars are appropriate where rideshare or taxi is impra

chunks.text: Rental cars are appropriate where rideshare or taxi is impractical (e.g., multiple stops over multiple days, rural destinations, multiple-passenger transport).

sections.section_heading: Vehicle category

chunks.text: Vehicle category.

Compact or mid-size vehicles are the default. Full-size vehicles are reimbursable only when transporting 3 or more passengers or significant equipment. Premium, luxury, and SUV categories are not reimbursable unless required by terrain or capacity, with manager approval.

sections.section_heading: Insurance

chunks.text: Insurance.

Decline collision damage waiver (CDW) and liability insurance offered by the rental company when traveling within the United States — the Company's commercial auto policy covers these. Outside the United States, accept the rental company's CDW.

sections.section_heading: Fuel. Refuel before returning the vehicle

chunks.text: Fuel. Refuel before returning the vehicle.
Pre-paid fuel options and per-gallon refueling charges from the rental company are not reimbursable.

sections.section_heading: Use of a personal vehicle for business purposes is reimburse

chunks.text: Use of a personal vehicle for business purposes is reimbursed at the IRS standard mileage rate, currently $0.67 per mile for 2025.

sections.section_heading: Reimbursement covers business mileage only

chunks.text: Reimbursement covers business mileage only. Commuting mileage (home to regular office) is not reimbursable.

sections.section_heading: Mileage must be documented with starting point, destination,

chunks.text: Mileage must be documented with starting point, destination, business purpose, and total miles for each trip.

sections.section_heading: Public transit (subway, bus, light rail, commuter rail) is r

chunks.text: Public transit (subway, bus, light rail, commuter rail) is reimbursable at actual cost.

sections.section_heading: Multi-day or weekly passes are reimbursable if they are econ

chunks.text: Multi-day or weekly passes are reimbursable if they are economically equivalent to or cheaper than the sum of individual fares for the business travel period.

sections.section_heading: Parking at business destinations, hotels, and airports durin

chunks.text: Parking at business destinations, hotels, and airports during business travel is reimbursable at actual cost.

sections.section_heading: Long-term airport parking should be used for trips of 3 days

chunks.text: Long-term airport parking should be used for trips of 3 days or more where the cost is lower than ground transportation alternatives.

sections.section_heading: Valet parking is reimbursable only where it is the only avai

chunks.text: Valet parking is reimbursable only where it is the only available option or where the price difference vs self-parking is under $15.

sections.section_heading: Tolls

chunks.text: Highway tolls incurred during business travel are reimbursable at actual cost.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Rideshare — A peer-to-peer ground transportation service such as Uber, Lyft, or regional equivalent (Grab, Bolt, Didi, etc.). • Standard service category — The base service tier offered by the rideshare provider (UberX, Lyft Standard, etc.), as opposed to premium tiers. • IRS standard mileage rate — The per-mile rate published annually by the U.S. Internal Revenue Service for business use of a personal vehicle.

The current rate is in §4.1; it is updated by addendum when the IRS publishes a change. • Mid-size vehicle — A rental car category between compact and full-size, typically seating 5 adults. • Commuting mileage — Travel between the employee's home and their regular office, which is not reimbursable.

sections.section_heading: General rule

chunks.text: All expenses claimed for reimbursement must be supported by an original receipt, with the limited exceptions in Section 4.

sections.section_heading: Required receipt content

chunks.text: A valid receipt must show, at minimum: • Vendor / merchant name • Date of the transaction • Total amount and currency • Itemized breakdown of charges (for meals and entertainment, see Section 3) • Method of payment (last four digits of card sufficient; cash receipts require vendor stamp or signature)

sections.section_heading: Meal and entertainment expenses require an itemized receipt

chunks.text: Meal and entertainment expenses require an itemized receipt showing each item ordered, in addition to the summary tip-and-total receipt.

sections.section_heading: Where a vendor does not provide an itemized receipt (e.g., a

chunks.text: Where a vendor does not provide an itemized receipt (e.g., a food truck with hand-written totals only), the employee must record an itemization on the expense report.

sections.section_heading: Itemization is necessary to verify compliance with the Meals

chunks.text: Itemization is necessary to verify compliance with the Meals & Entertainment Policy (TEP-002) and the Alcohol Policy (TEP-003), specifically the requirement that alcohol be itemized separately.

sections.section_heading: Under-$25 threshold

chunks.text: Under-$25 threshold. Individual expenses of less than $25 do not require a receipt, except for the following categories which require receipts regardless of amount: any alcohol charge, any tip exceeding $20, any cash advance disbursement.

sections.section_heading: Lost receipts

chunks.text: Lost receipts. If a receipt is lost or unavailable, the employee may submit a Missing Receipt Affidavit (available in Concur) attesting to the expense. Affidavits may not be used more than twice per quarter per employee.

sections.section_heading: Per-diem travel

chunks.text: Per-diem travel. When traveling on a per-diem basis (see TEP-008), individual meal receipts are not required for meals covered by the per-diem.

sections.section_heading: Electronic receipts (email confirmations, PDF receipts, mobi

chunks.text: Electronic receipts (email confirmations, PDF receipts, mobile receipt apps) are acceptable.

sections.section_heading: Photographs of paper receipts taken with a mobile camera are

chunks.text: Photographs of paper receipts taken with a mobile camera are acceptable provided all required content is clearly legible.

sections.section_heading: Translated receipts: receipts in languages other than Englis

chunks.text: Translated receipts: receipts in languages other than English do not require translation, but the expense report line description must be in English.

sections.section_heading: Each receipt submitted must match the line item it supports

chunks.text: Each receipt submitted must match the line item it supports. The amount on the receipt must match the amount claimed (subject to small rounding differences of under $1 due to currency conversion).

sections.section_heading: Amount mismatch

chunks.text: Amount mismatch.

Where the receipt amount differs materially from the amount claimed, the line item will be flagged. Reimbursement is the lesser of the two unless the employee provides a satisfactory explanation (e.g., currency conversion, separate gratuity charge).

sections.section_heading: Receipt-line orphans

chunks.text: Receipt-line orphans. Line items without a supporting receipt (other than those covered by Section 4) will be flagged and may be non-reimbursable.

sections.section_heading: Retention

chunks.text: The Company retains submitted receipts for 7 years per applicable tax and audit requirements. Employees should retain a personal copy of any unusual or high-value receipts for the same period.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Receipt — A document issued by a vendor at the time of transaction evidencing payment. • Itemized receipt — A receipt showing each individual item or charge separately, as opposed to a summary-total receipt. • Missing Receipt Affidavit — A standard form (available in Concur) by which an employee attests to an expense for which no receipt is available. • Currency conversion — Translation of foreign-currency amounts to U.S. dollars at the rate posted by the Corporate Card issuer or, where not posted, the OANDA daily rate on the transaction date.

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy establishes the per-diem rates available to employees on extended business travel, as an alternative to itemized meal reimbursement.

sections.section_heading: Per-diem is an opt-in alternative for trips lasting 3 or mor

chunks.text: Per-diem is an opt-in alternative for trips lasting 3 or more consecutive nights away from the employee's home base. Trips shorter than 3 nights must use itemized meal reimbursement under TEP-002.

sections.section_heading: The choice between per-diem and itemized must be declared at

chunks.text: The choice between per-diem and itemized must be declared at the start of the trip and may not be changed mid-trip.

sections.section_heading: Per-diem covers meals and incidentals only

chunks.text: Per-diem covers meals and incidentals only. Lodging is reimbursed separately at actual cost per the Lodging Policy (TEP-004), and ground transportation is reimbursed at actual cost per TEP-006.

sections.section_heading: Daily rates

chunks.text: Per-diem rates by city tier (matching the lodging tier definitions in TEP-004 §3): Tier Daily rate (USD) Tier 1 $90 Tier 2 $70 Tier 3 $55 Tier 4 (international) Set at trip approval

sections.section_heading: Departure day: 75% of the daily rate

chunks.text: Departure day: 75% of the daily rate.
4.2. Return day: 75% of the daily rate. 4.3. Full intermediate days: 100% of the daily rate.

sections.section_heading: Inclusions

chunks.text: The per-diem rate is intended to cover, in aggregate: • All three daily meals (breakfast, lunch, dinner) at typical mid-market quality • Non-alcoholic beverages • Reasonable incidentals (small purchases like bottled water, snacks, in-flight purchases under $20) • Tips to housekeeping and other service staff

sections.section_heading: Exclusions

chunks.text: The per-diem does not cover: • Alcoholic beverages (which remain subject to TEP-003 — reimbursable only for sanctioned client entertainment) • Client entertainment meals (which are reimbursed as itemized actual cost under TEP-002 §4, not from per-diem) • Lodging or ground transportation • Phone, internet, or business communications charges

sections.section_heading: Receipts under per-diem

chunks.text: Individual meal receipts are not required when per-diem is elected (per TEP-007 §4.3). The employee must, however, maintain documentation of trip duration and destination.

sections.section_heading: International travel

chunks.text: For international destinations not in Tier 1, Tier 2, or Tier 3, the per-diem rate is set at trip approval based on the U.S. Department of State foreign per-diem rates (M&IE component) for the destination city.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Per diem — A fixed daily allowance covering meals and incidentals, as an alternative to itemized reimbursement. • M&IE — Meals and Incidental Expenses, the component of U.S. federal per-diem rates that covers meal-and-incidental costs (as distinct from lodging). • Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3 / Tier 4 — City classifications matching the Lodging Policy (TEP-004 §3). • Intermediate day — A full day of business travel that is not the departure day or the return day.

sections.section_heading: Related documents

chunks.text: • TEP-001 Travel & Expense Policy — Overview • TEP-002 Meals and Entertainment Policy (alternative to per-diem) • TEP-004 Lodging Policy (parallel city tiers) • TEP-007 Receipt Requirements Policy (waiver under per-diem) • TEP-013 International Travel Policy (international per-diem)

chunks.text: Employee Grades Reference Document: TEP-009 Version: 1.2 Effective Date: January 1, 2025 Owner: Human Resources, in coordination with Finance Operations

sections.section_heading: Purpose

chunks.text: This reference document defines the Company's employee grade system as used in the Travel & Expense Policy framework. It does not establish compensation or organizational authority — those are governed by separate HR documents.

sections.section_heading: Grade ladder

chunks.text: Grade Typical title Approval authority Associate / Grade 1 None Coordinator Grade 2 Senior Associate None Grade 3 Specialist Approves own travel up to $500 Grade 4 Senior Specialist Approves own travel up to $1,000 Grade 5 Manager Approves direct-report travel up to $2,000 Grade 6 Senior Manager Approves direct-report travel up to $3,000 Grade 7 Director Approves up to $5,000; first VP-level approval Grade 8 Senior Director Approves up to $10,000 Approves up to $25,000; required approver for international Grade 9 Vice President travel Grade SVP / C-suite Unlimited within budget authority

sections.section_heading: 3. Application to travel approvals

chunks.text: 3. Application to travel approvals

sections.section_heading: Per the Travel & Expense Policy (TEP-001 §4), submissions ar

chunks.text: Per the Travel & Expense Policy (TEP-001 §4), submissions are subject to approval thresholds. The grade ladder above defines who holds the relevant approval authority.

sections.section_heading: VP approval (referred to throughout the policy suite) means

chunks.text: VP approval (referred to throughout the policy suite) means approval from a Grade 9 employee or above.

sections.section_heading: Director approval means approval from a Grade 7 employee or

chunks.text: Director approval means approval from a Grade 7 employee or above.

sections.section_heading: Manager approval means approval from the employee's direct m

chunks.text: Manager approval means approval from the employee's direct manager, regardless of that manager's grade, provided the manager holds approval authority for the relevant amount.

sections.section_heading: The employee's grade does not change the applicable per-diem

chunks.text: The employee's grade does not change the applicable per-diem rate or lodging tier cap. All employees on the same trip are subject to the same caps.

sections.section_heading: Grade does affect approval thresholds (Section 3) but not en

chunks.text: Grade does affect approval thresholds (Section 3) but not entitlement to expense categories.

sections.section_heading: Acting capacity

chunks.text: Employees acting in a temporary higher-grade capacity (e.g., a Senior Manager covering for a Director on leave) may exercise the approval authority of the higher grade only when formally designated in writing by the absent manager's manager.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Grade — A numerical classification of an employee's level in the Company hierarchy.

Grades are independent of job family and title. • Approval authority — The maximum dollar amount of expense the employee may approve for others. An employee always retains full approval authority for their own expenses subject to compliance. • Direct report — An employee whose primary reporting line is to the approver, as reflected in Workday. • Acting capacity — A temporary formal designation of higher-grade authority during the incumbent's absence.

sections.section_heading: Corporate Cards are issued to employees at Grade 4 and above

chunks.text: Corporate Cards are issued to employees at Grade 4 and above, and to any employee whose role requires regular business travel or client entertainment regardless of grade, subject to manager nomination and Finance Operations approval.

sections.section_heading: Contractors and temporary employees are not eligible for Cor

chunks.text: Contractors and temporary employees are not eligible for Corporate Cards.

sections.section_heading: The Corporate Card may be used only for business expenses th

chunks.text: The Corporate Card may be used only for business expenses that are reimbursable under the Travel & Expense Policy framework.

sections.section_heading: Personal use of the Corporate Card is prohibited, even where

chunks.text: Personal use of the Corporate Card is prohibited, even where the employee intends to repay the Company.

sections.section_heading: The Corporate Card should be used as the primary payment met

chunks.text: The Corporate Card should be used as the primary payment method for business travel and entertainment. Use of a personal card in lieu of the Corporate Card requires: • A documented business reason (e.g., the Corporate Card was declined, the vendor does not accept the card brand) • Manager approval at the time of reimbursement • Submission of the personal card receipt with the expense report

sections.section_heading: All Corporate Card charges must be reconciled and submitted

chunks.text: All Corporate Card charges must be reconciled and submitted as part of an expense report within

sections.section_heading: days of the statement date, even if the charge is suspect or

chunks.text: days of the statement date, even if the charge is suspect or under dispute.

sections.section_heading: Unreconciled charges over 60 days old will be deducted from

chunks.text: Unreconciled charges over 60 days old will be deducted from the employee's payroll without further notice, in accordance with the Cardholder Agreement signed at card issuance.

sections.section_heading: The Corporate Card may not be shared, lent, or used by anyon

chunks.text: The Corporate Card may not be shared, lent, or used by anyone other than the named cardholder.

sections.section_heading: Lost or stolen cards must be reported to the card issuer and

chunks.text: Lost or stolen cards must be reported to the card issuer and to Finance Operations within 24 hours of discovery.

sections.section_heading: The card PIN may not be written down or stored alongside the

chunks.text: The card PIN may not be written down or stored alongside the card.

sections.section_heading: Prohibited charges

chunks.text: The following are explicitly prohibited on the Corporate Card and constitute a policy violation even if subsequently disputed or refunded: • Cash advances (except where authorized for international travel) • Personal purchases of any kind • Gambling, adult entertainment, or political contributions • Alcohol in violation of TEP-003 (Alcohol Policy) • Purchases on behalf of family members, friends, or third parties

sections.section_heading: Charges the cardholder believes to be fraudulent or in error

chunks.text: Charges the cardholder believes to be fraudulent or in error must be disputed with the card issuer within the issuer's dispute window (typically 60 days from the statement date).

sections.section_heading: Disputed charges still appear on the expense report

chunks.text: Disputed charges still appear on the expense report. The cardholder should mark them as "Disputed — Awaiting Resolution" and submit supporting documentation.

sections.section_heading: Card return

chunks.text: The Corporate Card must be returned to Finance Operations on the employee's last working day. Charges incurred after the last working day are the employee's personal liability and will be recovered.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Corporate Card — A commercial credit card issued in the employee's name under the Company's commercial card program with the card issuer. • Cardholder Agreement — The contract signed by the employee at card issuance establishing personal liability for misuse. • Reconciliation — The process of matching each card statement charge to a submitted expense report line item with appropriate documentation. • Dispute — A formal challenge to a card charge filed with the card issuer alleging fraud or error.

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy governs the giving and receiving of gifts, hospitality, and entertainment in the context of business relationships with clients, vendors, prospects, and other external parties. It supplements the Code of Conduct (COC-001) and the Meals and Entertainment Policy (TEP-002).

sections.section_heading: Gifts and entertainment should be modest, infrequent, and cl

chunks.text: Gifts and entertainment should be modest, infrequent, and clearly intended to maintain a business relationship — not to influence a specific business decision.

sections.section_heading: Cash and cash equivalents (including gift cards exceeding $2

chunks.text: Cash and cash equivalents (including gift cards exceeding $25) may never be accepted, regardless of source.

sections.section_heading: Non-cash gifts may be accepted from a single source up to a

chunks.text: Non-cash gifts may be accepted from a single source up to a value of $50 per gift and $200 cumulatively per calendar year.

sections.section_heading: Gifts valued above $50 must be declined and returned, with a

chunks.text: Gifts valued above $50 must be declined and returned, with a polite note referencing this policy. Where return is impractical (e.g., perishable items, hand-delivered ceremonial gifts in cultures where refusal is offensive), the employee should accept the item and turn it over to HR for donation or shared distribution.

sections.section_heading: Promotional items of nominal value (branded pens, notepads,

chunks.text: Promotional items of nominal value (branded pens, notepads, bottles of water) are not considered gifts under this policy.

sections.section_heading: Client gifts are permitted up to $100 per recipient per cale

chunks.text: Client gifts are permitted up to $100 per recipient per calendar year, aggregated across all gifts to that individual.

sections.section_heading: Gifts to government officials, public-sector employees, or r

chunks.text: Gifts to government officials, public-sector employees, or representatives of state-owned enterprises are prohibited unless specifically approved by the General Counsel in writing.

sections.section_heading: Cash and cash-equivalent gifts to clients are prohibited und

chunks.text: Cash and cash-equivalent gifts to clients are prohibited under all circumstances.

sections.section_heading: Client entertainment (meals, events, sporting events, etc.)

chunks.text: Client entertainment (meals, events, sporting events, etc.) is governed by the per-person caps in the Meals and Entertainment Policy (TEP-002 §4).

sections.section_heading: Entertainment that includes spouses or family members of eit

chunks.text: Entertainment that includes spouses or family members of either party requires VP-level approval and must have a clear business rationale.

sections.section_heading: Tickets to events (concerts, sporting events, theater) provi

chunks.text: Tickets to events (concerts, sporting events, theater) provided to employees by a third party must be declared if the face value exceeds $50. If an employee is invited to attend an event in the company of the third party (i.e., the third party is also attending and there is a business discussion), the event is treated as entertainment rather than a gift, and the value limits in 3.2 do not apply — but the engagement should still be reasonable in frequency.

sections.section_heading: Nothing in this policy authorizes, condones, or permits any

chunks.text: Nothing in this policy authorizes, condones, or permits any payment or thing of value provided with the intent of influencing an official act, securing an improper advantage, or rewarding the recipient for past favorable treatment. Such conduct violates the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, the UK Bribery Act, and equivalent laws in other jurisdictions where the Company operates.

sections.section_heading: Suspected bribery should be reported immediately to the Gene

chunks.text: Suspected bribery should be reported immediately to the General Counsel or via the Ethics Hotline.

sections.section_heading: Gifts and entertainment that fall within the policy limits d

chunks.text: Gifts and entertainment that fall within the policy limits do not require individual disclosure.

sections.section_heading: Gifts and entertainment received above the limits in Section

chunks.text: Gifts and entertainment received above the limits in Section 3 must be reported to the employee's manager and to HR within 5 business days of receipt.

sections.section_heading: Business entertainment expensed to the Company is recorded o

chunks.text: Business entertainment expensed to the Company is recorded on the expense report and is subject to the Meals and Entertainment Policy (TEP-002).

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Gift — Anything of value transferred without commensurate consideration. Includes physical items, services, cash, gift cards, tickets, and other tangible benefits. • Promotional item — A branded item of nominal value (typically under $10) intended to promote the giver, such as pens, notepads, or branded merchandise. • Nominal value — Generally items under $10, distributed broadly rather than personally targeted. • Government official — Any official, employee, or representative of any government agency, state-owned enterprise, or public international organization, including political candidates and party officials.

chunks.text: • COC-001 Code of Conduct (§7 Gifts and Entertainment principles) • TEP-002 Meals and Entertainment Policy (per-person caps for entertainment) • TEP-003 Alcohol Policy • LEG-101 Whistleblower and Ethics Reporting Policy • LEG-201 Political Activity Policy (prohibitions on political contributions)

International Travel Policy Document: TEP-013 Version: 1.5 Effective Date: January 1, 2025 Owner: Finance Operations & Risk Management

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy supplements the general Travel & Expense Policy framework with rules specific to international business travel — defined as travel between countries, including travel between the United States and Canada.

sections.section_heading: All international travel requires advance written approval f

chunks.text: All international travel requires advance written approval from a Vice President (Grade 9) or above, in addition to the standard approval thresholds in TEP-001 §4.

sections.section_heading: Approval requests should be submitted at least 14 days prior

chunks.text: Approval requests should be submitted at least 14 days prior to departure to allow time for travel risk review, visa requirements, and currency arrangements.

sections.section_heading: International travel to destinations classified as high-risk

chunks.text: International travel to destinations classified as high-risk by Northwind's travel risk vendor (currently International SOS) requires additional security and Legal review.

sections.section_heading: Travel to countries under U.S

chunks.text: Travel to countries under U.S. economic sanctions is prohibited absent an Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) license.

sections.section_heading: International destinations not in Tier 1, Tier 2, or Tier 3

chunks.text: International destinations not in Tier 1, Tier 2, or Tier 3 of the Lodging Policy (TEP-004 §3) fall under Tier 4, where the rate cap is set at trip approval.

sections.section_heading: The approver should reference the U.S

chunks.text: The approver should reference the U.S. Department of State's per-diem rates for the destination as a benchmark.

sections.section_heading: International per-diem rates are set at the U.S

chunks.text: International per-diem rates are set at the U.S. Department of State M&IE rate for the destination city, at the time of trip approval.

sections.section_heading: International per-diem may be elected for trips of 3 or more

chunks.text: International per-diem may be elected for trips of 3 or more nights, consistent with TEP-008.

sections.section_heading: Business class is permitted on international segments of 10

chunks.text: Business class is permitted on international segments of 10 hours or more with VP approval, per TEP-005 §2.3.

sections.section_heading: Premium economy is the default for international segments of

chunks.text: Premium economy is the default for international segments of 6 hours or more.

sections.section_heading: Stopovers added for personal reasons are at the employee's e

chunks.text: Stopovers added for personal reasons are at the employee's expense, with a comparison quote required at booking.

sections.section_heading: Currency conversion

chunks.text: Currency conversion. Expenses incurred in foreign currency should be reported in U.S. dollars at the exchange rate on the date of the transaction, using the rate posted by the Corporate Card issuer where applicable, or the OANDA daily rate otherwise.

sections.section_heading: Foreign transaction fees charged by the Corporate Card issue

chunks.text: Foreign transaction fees charged by the Corporate Card issuer are reimbursable as a separate line item, not bundled into the underlying expense.

sections.section_heading: Cash advances for international travel are permitted in limi

chunks.text: Cash advances for international travel are permitted in limited amounts and require advance approval from Finance Operations.

sections.section_heading: Visa fees, visa-application courier fees, and required immun

chunks.text: Visa fees, visa-application courier fees, and required immunizations for business travel are reimbursable at actual cost.

sections.section_heading: Passport application or renewal fees are not reimbursable un

chunks.text: Passport application or renewal fees are not reimbursable unless the employee is required to obtain a passport solely for a specific business trip (e.g., previously did not need one and would not need one for future personal use). Such cases are rare and require VP approval.

sections.section_heading: Alcohol

chunks.text: The Alcohol Policy (TEP-003) applies to international travel with the modification noted in TEP-003 §6: where local law restricts alcohol, no alcohol is reimbursable regardless of business context.

sections.section_heading: Dependents

chunks.text: Travel costs for spouses, dependents, or other companions on international trips are never reimbursable, per the general rule in TEP-005 §6.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • International travel — Travel involving a border crossing, including U.S.-Canada and U.S.- Mexico travel. • High-risk destination — A country classified as elevated risk by the Company's contracted travel risk vendor (currently International SOS); see SEC-301. • OFAC license — Authorization issued by the U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control to engage in otherwise-prohibited transactions in sanctioned jurisdictions. • Stopover — A scheduled break in an itinerary, typically more than 24 hours, not necessary for the routing itself.

sections.section_heading: Related documents

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This policy governs employee attendance at external conferences, training events, industry summits, and similar professional development activities involving travel or significant Company expense.

sections.section_heading: Conference attendance must be pre-approved in writing by the

chunks.text: Conference attendance must be pre-approved in writing by the employee's manager. Approval requests should include: • The conference name, dates, and location • Estimated total cost (registration + travel + lodging + meals) • Business justification — how the conference relates to the employee's role, team objectives, or career development plan • Names of any other Northwind employees attending the same conference

sections.section_heading: Where the total cost exceeds $5,000, Director-level (Grade 7

chunks.text: Where the total cost exceeds $5,000, Director-level (Grade 7) approval is required. Where it exceeds $10,000 or involves international travel, VP-level (Grade 9) approval is required, per TEP-001 §4.

sections.section_heading: Conference registration fees are reimbursable at the standar

chunks.text: Conference registration fees are reimbursable at the standard attendee rate.

sections.section_heading: Premium passes (VIP, all-access, executive tracks) are reimb

chunks.text: Premium passes (VIP, all-access, executive tracks) are reimbursable only when they provide demonstrable business value not available at the standard rate (e.g., access to specific workshops critical to the employee's role).

sections.section_heading: Early-bird registration should be elected whenever the confe

chunks.text: Early-bird registration should be elected whenever the conference offers it and the trip is planned far enough in advance.

sections.section_heading: Travel and lodging

chunks.text: Travel and lodging to and from the conference are reimbursed under the general framework (TEP-004 Lodging, TEP-005 Air Travel, TEP-006 Ground Transportation).

sections.section_heading: Meals included with registration

chunks.text: Meals included with registration. Where the conference registration fee includes meals (most full-day conferences include lunch; many include breakfast and reception meals), no separate reimbursement is available for those meals. The employee should consume the included meals.

sections.section_heading: Meals not included with registration are reimbursed under th

chunks.text: Meals not included with registration are reimbursed under the standard Meals and Entertainment Policy (TEP-002) per-meal caps.

sections.section_heading: Off-site dinners with conference colleagues are not automati

chunks.text: Off-site dinners with conference colleagues are not automatically client entertainment. They are treated as solo or team-only meals (TEP-002 §2) unless attended by an external client or prospect.

sections.section_heading: Where an employee attends a conference together with a clien

chunks.text: Where an employee attends a conference together with a client (e.g., bringing a client to an industry event), the related entertainment is governed by the Meals and Entertainment Policy (TEP-002 §4) and may carry higher caps if VP approval is in place.

sections.section_heading: Employees may extend the trip for personal reasons (e.g., a

chunks.text: Employees may extend the trip for personal reasons (e.g., a weekend before or after the conference). The Company pays only for the business portion of the trip.

sections.section_heading: Compare-quote requirement: where extending the trip increase

chunks.text: Compare-quote requirement: where extending the trip increases the airfare, the employee must provide a comparison quote at booking showing the cost for the business portion only, and the difference is the employee's responsibility.

sections.section_heading: Employees attending an approved conference are expected to s

chunks.text: Employees attending an approved conference are expected to share key takeaways with their team within 30 days of return. A short written summary (one page, or a presentation) is appropriate; the format is at the manager's discretion.

sections.section_heading: Conference materials, slides, and recordings provided by the

chunks.text: Conference materials, slides, and recordings provided by the conference may be shared internally consistent with the conference's attendee terms.

sections.section_heading: If an approved conference is cancelled or the employee can n

chunks.text: If an approved conference is cancelled or the employee can no longer attend, the employee should pursue refunds of registration and travel costs to the extent practicable.

sections.section_heading: Non-refundable costs from a cancelled conference are absorbe

chunks.text: Non-refundable costs from a cancelled conference are absorbed by the Company provided the cancellation was for a legitimate reason (manager decision, illness, business priority change).

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Conference — A multi-session organized industry event involving travel or significant Company expense, typically including paid registration. • Conference deliverables — A short written summary or presentation prepared by the attendee within 30 days of return. • Early-bird registration — A registration tier offered at a discount for early commitment, typically 30-90 days before the event.

sections.section_heading: Purpose

chunks.text: This protocol describes the risk assessment, preparation, and ongoing support that applies to international business travel. Its purpose is to protect the safety, security, and well-being of traveling employees and to enable the Company to fulfill its duty-of-care obligations.

sections.section_heading: Scope

chunks.text: This protocol applies to all international business travel — defined as travel involving a border crossing in connection with Company business.

It does not modify reimbursement rules, which are governed by the International Travel Policy (TEP-013) and related travel & expense documents. This protocol does not apply to domestic travel except where a specific destination has been identified as elevated risk.

sections.section_heading: The Company contracts with International SOS for travel risk

chunks.text: The Company contracts with International SOS for travel risk intelligence and 24/7 medical and security assistance. All international travelers are enrolled in International SOS automatically based on travel bookings made through Concur Travel.

sections.section_heading: The International SOS Member Number for Company travelers is

chunks.text: The International SOS Member Number for Company travelers is available in the Travel Portal.

sections.section_heading: Country risk classification is published by International SO

chunks.text: Country risk classification is published by International SOS. The current Company-recognized tiers are: Tier Description Action required Most of Western Europe, USA/Canada, Australia, NZ, Low Standard travel booking Japan, Singapore Standard booking + traveler Medium Most of Latin America, Eastern Europe, much of Asia briefing Tier Description Action required Designated countries with elevated security, health, or VP approval + Risk Management High political risk briefing Extreme Active conflict zones, sanctioned countries Generally prohibited

sections.section_heading: Travel to High-tier destinations requires: - Vice President

chunks.text: Travel to High-tier destinations requires: - Vice President approval (Grade 9+) per TEP-013 §2 - A pre-travel risk briefing with Risk Management - Confirmation of International SOS enrollment - An itinerary submitted to Risk Management

sections.section_heading: Travel to Extreme-tier destinations is generally prohibited

chunks.text: Travel to Extreme-tier destinations is generally prohibited and requires CFO and General Counsel approval, with a documented business necessity that could not be addressed remotely.

sections.section_heading: Travelers are responsible for obtaining required visas

chunks.text: Travelers are responsible for obtaining required visas. Visa fees are reimbursable under TEP-013 §8.

sections.section_heading: Immunizations recommended for the destination should be obta

chunks.text: Immunizations recommended for the destination should be obtained well in advance of travel. The Company will reimburse for required immunizations.

sections.section_heading: A copy of the traveler's passport and itinerary should be le

chunks.text: A copy of the traveler's passport and itinerary should be left with a trusted contact at home.

sections.section_heading: Communication

chunks.text: Communication. Travelers should maintain regular communication with their home office and family.
International SOS may be used for routine status calls in elevated-risk destinations.

sections.section_heading: Lodging

chunks.text: Lodging. Stay in vetted lodging — preferably international hotel chains with consistent security standards.
Confirm the lodging is on the International SOS approved list for High-tier destinations.

sections.section_heading: Local transportation

chunks.text: Local transportation. Vetted ground transportation should be arranged in advance for airport transfers in High-tier destinations.

sections.section_heading: Documents

chunks.text: Documents. Carry the passport, secondary photo ID, and a copy of the visa. Keep these secure (hotel safe when not needed; document holder when needed).

sections.section_heading: Health

chunks.text: Health.
Follow standard precautions for food and water in the destination. Pack a basic first-aid kit. Identify the nearest International SOS-recommended medical facility.

sections.section_heading: Medical emergency

chunks.text: Medical emergency. Call local emergency services and notify International SOS, who can coordinate evacuation, second opinions, and home-office notification.

sections.section_heading: Security incident (loss, theft, harassment, civil unrest)

chunks.text: Security incident (loss, theft, harassment, civil unrest). Move to safe location and call International SOS for assistance.

sections.section_heading: Detention or arrest

chunks.text: Detention or arrest. Identify yourself, do not resist, and request to contact the U.S. Embassy (or relevant home-country embassy) and International SOS. Notify Risk Management as soon as possible.

sections.section_heading: Death or serious injury

chunks.text: Death or serious injury. Notify International SOS first, who will support family notification, repatriation, and Company coordination.

sections.section_heading: Follow the Mobile Device Acceptable Use Standard (SEC-204) f

chunks.text: Follow the Mobile Device Acceptable Use Standard (SEC-204) for handling devices internationally, including the recommendation to use loaner devices in high-risk destinations.

sections.section_heading: Border searches of electronic devices are increasingly commo

chunks.text: Border searches of electronic devices are increasingly common. Travelers should assume that devices may be inspected and should not carry sensitive data on the device when crossing borders.

sections.section_heading: Travel to countries under U.S

chunks.text: Travel to countries under U.S. economic sanctions (currently: Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, and certain regions of Ukraine and Russia) is prohibited absent an OFAC license. Travel approval requests for these destinations are routed to General Counsel.

sections.section_heading: Sanctions screening is part of the pre-travel review for bor

chunks.text: Sanctions screening is part of the pre-travel review for borderline destinations.

sections.section_heading: Combining personal travel with a business trip is acceptable

chunks.text: Combining personal travel with a business trip is acceptable. Reimbursement is only for the business portion (TEP-005 §6.2, TEP-013 §6.3).

sections.section_heading: The risk and security protections in this document apply onl

chunks.text: The risk and security protections in this document apply only to the business portion of the trip. International SOS coverage during the personal portion is at the traveler's election; many home insurance and credit card products provide travel medical coverage.

sections.section_heading: After return from a High-tier destination, travelers should

chunks.text: After return from a High-tier destination, travelers should debrief with Risk Management within 5 business days. Debriefs help update the Company's risk understanding.

sections.section_heading: If symptoms of illness develop within 14 days of return from

chunks.text: If symptoms of illness develop within 14 days of return from certain destinations (per CDC or WHO advisories), notify HR before returning to the office; remote work may be advisable.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • High-tier destination — A country categorized as elevated risk per International SOS. • OFAC — U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control; administers economic sanctions programs. • International SOS — The Company's contracted travel risk and medical assistance vendor.

Code of Conduct Document: COC-001 Version: 4.0 Effective Date: January 1, 2025 Owner: Human Resources & Legal

sections.section_heading: Purpose

chunks.text: This Code of Conduct sets out the ethical and professional standards expected of all Northwind Logistics employees, contractors, and authorized representatives. Compliance is a condition of employment.

sections.section_heading: Employees are expected to be honest in all dealings with col

chunks.text: Employees are expected to be honest in all dealings with colleagues, clients, vendors, regulators, and the public.

sections.section_heading: Misrepresentation of facts, falsification of records (includ

chunks.text: Misrepresentation of facts, falsification of records (including expense reports, time sheets, or invoices), or concealment of material information is grounds for immediate termination.

sections.section_heading: Northwind is committed to a workplace free from harassment,

chunks.text: Northwind is committed to a workplace free from harassment, discrimination, and bullying. Employees are expected to treat colleagues with respect regardless of race, ethnicity, gender, sexual orientation, religion, age, disability, or any other protected characteristic.

sections.section_heading: Concerns or complaints should be raised with the employee's

chunks.text: Concerns or complaints should be raised with the employee's manager, HR, or via the anonymous ethics hotline.

sections.section_heading: Employees must disclose, in writing to their manager, any ac

chunks.text: Employees must disclose, in writing to their manager, any actual or potential conflict of interest. Examples include: • Personal or family relationships with vendors, clients, or job candidates • Outside business interests that compete with the Company • Investments in Company clients or vendors above 1% ownership

sections.section_heading: Decisions made on behalf of the Company must be made in the

chunks.text: Decisions made on behalf of the Company must be made in the Company's interest. Where a conflict exists, the employee must recuse themselves from the relevant decision.

sections.section_heading: Employees have access to confidential information about the

chunks.text: Employees have access to confidential information about the Company, its clients, its strategies, and its employees. This information must not be disclosed outside the Company except as required by the employee's role.

sections.section_heading: Company resources (funds, equipment, time, intellectual prop

chunks.text: Company resources (funds, equipment, time, intellectual property) are to be used for business purposes. Limited and occasional personal use of equipment (e.g., personal email at lunch) is acceptable; sustained personal use is not.

sections.section_heading: Expense reports submitted under the Travel & Expense Policy

chunks.text: Expense reports submitted under the Travel & Expense Policy framework (TEP-001 et seq.) must accurately reflect business expenses actually incurred. Inflated, fabricated, or duplicated expense claims are treated as falsification of records under Section 2.

sections.section_heading: Gifts and entertainment

chunks.text: See the Gifts and Entertainment Policy (TEP-012) for specific rules. The principles in this section apply:

sections.section_heading: Gifts and entertainment should never influence, or appear to

chunks.text: Gifts and entertainment should never influence, or appear to influence, business decisions.

sections.section_heading: Employees should refuse gifts whose acceptance would create

chunks.text: Employees should refuse gifts whose acceptance would create the appearance of impropriety, regardless of monetary value.

sections.section_heading: Compliance with law

chunks.text: Employees must comply with all applicable laws and regulations in jurisdictions where the Company operates, including but not limited to anti-bribery, anti-money-laundering, antitrust, employment, tax, and securities laws.

sections.section_heading: Employees who become aware of a potential violation of this

chunks.text: Employees who become aware of a potential violation of this Code are expected to report it through one of the following channels: • Direct manager • Skip-level manager • Human Resources • General Counsel • Anonymous Ethics Hotline (operated by a third party)

sections.section_heading: The Company prohibits retaliation against any employee who r

chunks.text: The Company prohibits retaliation against any employee who reports a concern in good faith, even if the concern proves unfounded after investigation.

sections.section_heading: Enforcement

chunks.text: Violations of this Code are subject to disciplinary action up to and including termination. Certain violations may also be reported to law enforcement or regulatory authorities.

sections.section_heading: Definitions

chunks.text: • Conflict of interest — A situation in which an employee's personal interests, relationships, or outside activities could compromise — or appear to compromise — their judgment, objectivity, or loyalty to the Company. • Confidential information — Non-public information about the Company, its clients, employees, vendors, or strategy, the disclosure of which could harm the Company or any of its stakeholders. • Material violation — A violation of this Code that has, or could have, a significant effect on the Company, its operations, its reputation, or compliance with law. • Good faith — Acting on a reasonable belief in the truth or significance of one's statements or actions.
